video_title: America's Trillion-Dollar Race to Catch Chinese Miners
gist: Two-part show: a markets segment on single-stock fragility at index highs, the SpaceX mega-IPO sucking liquidity from telecom/defense, and rising market 'gamification'; followed by an interview with Metals Royalty Company (NASDAQ: TMCR) CEO Brian Paes-Braga arguing the US is in inning one of a US-led critical-minerals commodity super-cycle, with China controlling 19 of 20 energy-critical minerals and refining 30-99% of supply.

themes.theme: Single-stock fragility under a calm VIX

insights.claim: Broadcom fell 16.5% the day after earnings despite solid metrics, because its AI revenue guidance came in below expectations — signaling that mega-cap names that miss on the specific metric the market is watching will be punished immediately.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: At index highs with a sub-16 VIX, position sizing should account for 15%+ single-name gap risk on any guidance miss; the index's calm masks violent dispersion.
insights.quote: The stock was down 16.5%. This was a 2.3 trillion market cap company.

insights.claim: Broadcom's plunge did not spread — direct competitor Marvell rose 4%, Taiwan Semi rose, and customer Google rose nearly 4% the same day — implying the market read the miss as Broadcom-specific rather than a sector-wide AI demand crack.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Use the non-contagion as a tell: AI capex demand thesis stays intact; the selloff was an entry, not a regime change, for the broader complex.

insights.claim: Guy Adami reads the Broadcom retrace (a full give-back of its mid-May run from ~415 to ~495) as a possible 'first domino' and warns that large gap-up reactions to earnings (Cisco, Intel, Dell, IBM) are historically a late-cycle, not early-cycle, market characteristic.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Treat outsized upside earnings gaps on mega-caps as a caution flag on cycle maturity rather than confirmation of a new leg higher.
insights.quote: That to me is not particularly bullish.

insights.claim: The SEC's repeal of the pattern-day-trading rule, alongside higher retail margin usage, is expected to increase intraday single-stock volatility — especially in the highest-momentum names.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Expect fatter intraday tails in momentum leaders; options/vol strategies on those names may be underpriced relative to realized single-stock moves.

insights.claim: Google is positioned to win the AI race with a still-reasonable valuation, having converted prior weaknesses (lagging Gemini, GCP behind Azure/AWS, no TPU story) into strengths; existential search-disruption fears have receded.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Among mega-cap AI plays, GOOGL screens as the rare 'win the race at a reasonable multiple' name to overweight.

insights.claim: Google upsized a capital raise to $85 billion — anchored by Berkshire Hathaway taking $10 billion — signaling a shift among cash-rich AI builders toward selling equity/debt rather than depleting cash flow, which implicitly says management views the AI buildout as a better return than buying back its own stock.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Equity issuance by net-cash AI names dilutes future EPS and reduces buyback support — a structural headwind to per-share metrics even as the businesses grow.
insights.quote: My investments though that I am going to make in this are going to be a better long-term investment than my equity right here, right now.

themes.theme: SpaceX IPO as a liquidity vacuum

insights.claim: SpaceX is expected to IPO around June 12th as potentially the largest IPO in history, with Elon Musk personally setting the price, ~30% allocated to retail, 36 banks marketing it, and underwriting fees compressed to ~1% versus a normal 5-6%.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A mega-cap, small-float IPO with index-inclusion mechanics forces incremental buying; the aftermarket trade, not the pricing, is the signal to watch for broader market risk appetite.
insights.quote: He is setting the price as they said in the movie Trading Places.

insights.claim: Telecom (T-Mobile, AT&T, Verizon) and defense primes (Lockheed, Northrop, RTX) are trading poorly near 52-week lows — down 15-30% — as the market pre-emptively prices SpaceX/Starlink as an existential threat rather than waiting to confirm it.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Starlink-exposed telecom and SpaceX-threatened defense names are being de-rated on narrative; watch for overshoot/mean-reversion or a structural short thesis depending on Starlink execution.
insights.quote: We're not going to wait around to find out.

insights.claim: Lockheed Martin has fallen from an all-time high near $700 (made in March) to just north of $500, and AT&T has round-tripped from $22 in late January up to ~$29 and back to $22 (its lowest since January 2025), illustrating both the defense de-rating and extreme single-name volatility.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Concrete levels for the de-rated cohort: LMT ~500 off ~700, T at ~22 — reference points for entry/exit if the SpaceX-threat narrative reverses.

insights.claim: A wave of mega-cap private IPOs — SpaceX, xAI, Anthropic, OpenAI — needs public markets to access new incremental buyers and unlock debt issuance, but the cash to buy them must rotate out of existing positions.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Anticipate forced rotation/funding pressure on incumbents as the IPO pipeline drains liquidity; identify likely sources of sold stock.
insights.quote: Money's got to rotate from somewhere.

insights.claim: Multiple investor-protection guardrails are being removed at once — pattern-day-trading rule repealed, CFPB abandoned, lightly-regulated prediction markets/event contracts proliferating, elevated margin usage — turning prior market-structure tailwinds into potential headwinds.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Deregulation feeds near-term speculative froth but raises the odds of a disorderly downturn; treat the removed guardrails as a building tail risk.
insights.quote: All of a sudden I think a lot of the kind of guard rails have been pulled.

insights.claim: Adami argues the rule of law in markets will only return after a painful downturn that hurts investors, viewing such a reckoning as inevitable given the current 'casino'/gamification dynamic.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Positions for an eventual volatility/drawdown regime; the speaker sees mean-reversion in market discipline as a when-not-if event.
insights.quote: I think it's going to take a downturn where people get hurt, which I think there's an inevitability to that to bring those rules back in play.

insights.claim: Adami expects a geopolitical risk premium to persist in oil (still a $9-handle, i.e. ~$90) absent a meaningful ceasefire, since US adversaries have a vested interest in keeping prices elevated to weaken the US into the July 4th 250th anniversary.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Stay constructive on energy commodities and equities; expect a politically-driven 'push-pull' keeping oil bid into summer.
insights.quote: I don't think the energy trade in terms of the commodity is over by any stretch. Nor do I think it's over in the equities.

insights.claim: Elevated oil prices are already weighing on consumer discretionary spending, per commentary from retailers and fast-food chains noting consumers 'feeling the pinch.'
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Watch retail/QSR guidance as a real-time read on how high energy prices are eroding discretionary demand.

insights.claim: Paes-Braga argues the US is in 'inning one' of a new commodity super-cycle led by the United States re-industrializing — distinct from the 2001-2010 China/EM-led super-cycle — driven by national-security urgency around mineral independence.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Frames a multi-year secular bull case for miners and mineral-supply-chain assets; positions early in a cycle the speaker says has years to run.
insights.quote: We're at the brink of the start of this commodity super cycle and the metals royalty company finds itself right smack in the middle of all of it.

insights.claim: China controls 19 of 20 energy-related critical minerals and refines between 30% and 99% of global supply across metals, giving it security of supply and control of offtakes — the strategic gap the US must close.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the dependency; any US move toward refining/offtake independence is a multi-decade demand driver for Western mining and processing assets.
insights.quote: 19 of 20 critical minerals related to energy are controlled by China.

insights.claim: Paes-Braga draws a direct parallel between today's minerals push and the US shale revolution of ~10-15 years ago, arguing critical minerals are 'where oil was before shale' — a bipartisan, multi-hundred-billion-dollar national effort akin to breaking OPEC's grip.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Provides a historical analog (shale created huge new US industries and exporters) to size the potential investment opportunity in domestic minerals.
insights.quote: Where there's more challenges in the mining space is the time it takes to get these operations going.

insights.claim: The structural bottleneck is time, not just capital: average US mine development from discovery to build is 29 years, versus 45-60 days to drill an oil/gas well, leaving the US strategically exposed if China weaponizes supply.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: The 29-year lead time means existing/near-production assets and refining carry scarcity value; supply cannot respond quickly to demand or to a Chinese supply shock.
insights.quote: From discovery to a build of a mine, the average has been 29 years.

insights.claim: Iron ore independence — not headline rare earths — is the foundational opportunity; the Mesabi iron range in Minnesota that built American steel exemplifies the high-tonnage, jobs-and-taxes assets that matter most, while rare earths are a small slice of the market.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Redirects investor attention from hyped rare-earth names to large, economically fundamental commodities like iron ore where the real value sits.
insights.quote: It may not be the headlines of rare earth, but iron ore is fundamental to society.

insights.claim: Copper supply cannot meet demand: AI hyperscaler data centers use 40,000-50,000 tons of copper each, while Chile is not finding meaningfully more copper, Peru's new assets are early-stage, and the resource-rich Congo is hard to ramp due to infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: A constrained-supply, AI-driven-demand setup is structurally bullish copper; supply geography (Chile flat, Congo bottlenecked) limits the response.
insights.quote: These data centers... they're using anywhere from 40 to 50,000 tons of copper.

insights.claim: The US government has already committed ~$29 billion across agencies to critical minerals, and Paes-Braga expects total support to reach hundreds of billions — possibly low single-digit trillions — over the next decade given the urgency.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: A large, sustained public funding stream de-risks domestic mineral projects and is a direct tailwind for assets eligible for government support.
insights.quote: The last number I saw was they've already committed to $29 billion from different organizations within the government.

insights.claim: Government capital is flowing to the biggest projects because the policy theme is metal-by-metal independence, so scale plus location in tier-one jurisdictions (US, Canada, Australia) is the screen for which assets get backing.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Favor large-scale projects in tier-one jurisdictions — they command valuation premiums and are the likeliest recipients of government support.
insights.quote: We're going for the big ones we're going for the best.

insights.claim: Foreign industrial capital is already reshoring the supply chain — e.g., Korea Inc. announced a $7.2 billion refinery project in Tennessee, the first new US refinery of its kind in what may be decades.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence the reshoring thesis is funded and underway; refining/processing capacity in the US is a high-conviction build-out area.

insights.claim: Metals Royalty Company (NASDAQ: TMCR) provides 'last money in' capital to late-stage mining projects in exchange for 20-50 year royalty/streaming cash flows — e.g., $132.5 million into the Mesabi/Minnesota iron-ore project (up to ~$500 million total) that already had $2.5 billion spent and first production in H2 this year.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: A pure-play, US-listed vehicle to gain diversified critical-minerals exposure via asset-backed royalties rather than single-commodity mining risk.
insights.quote: We put $132.5 million in. We're going to probably deploy another $132.5 and maybe up to around half a billion.

insights.claim: Paes-Braga benchmarks the model to precious-metal royalty firms (Wheaton, Franco-Nevada) at $40-50 billion market caps trading at 20-40x cash flow with only ~10-250 employees, implying a high-multiple, high-free-cash-flow, low-headcount business he says no one is replicating in base/critical metals.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: The royalty model historically re-rates to premium multiples; TMCR is pitched as a first-mover applying it to critical/base metals — a white-space re-rating thesis.
insights.quote: It's white space. No one's doing this.

insights.claim: TMCR's portfolio already spans nickel, copper, cobalt, manganese and iron ore; management plans 3-5 transactions a year focused on the largest assets, mirroring how Wheaton/Franco-Nevada derive most value from ~20-30 core deals.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Defines the deployment cadence and diversification; a concentrated, big-asset strategy where a few deals drive most of the value.

insights.claim: A portfolio royalty structure mutes commodity-price risk: short-term free cash flow is affected by price drops but it is not existential as it is for a single-commodity miner, and falling prices let the firm do its best (distressed) deals.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: The model is counter-cyclical on deal-making — price downturns are an opportunity, not an existential threat, a key differentiator from pure miners.
insights.quote: During times where commodity prices roll over, we will probably do our best deals.

insights.claim: Paes-Braga's track record: his prior venture Lithium X bought a project for ~$4-6 million and sold it for ~$200 million in 17-18 months as lithium rose from $5,000 to $22,000 a ton, on a thesis that Tesla's EV demand would drive lithium — a template for the current minerals bet.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Establishes operator credibility and the demand-anticipation playbook now being applied to copper/iron ore/critical minerals.
insights.quote: Lithium went from 5,000 a ton to 22,000 a ton.

insights.claim: Paes-Braga frames three resource superpowers: Russia is self-sufficient (gas plus prolific nickel, aluminum, iron ore, gold), China went outside its borders and 'economically colonized' Africa over the past 25-30 years, and the US must now do the same — leveraging Latin America.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Geographic framework for where Western mineral capital must flow; Latin America is the US's analog to China-in-Africa.
insights.quote: China had to go outside their borders to maintain their economic position in the world and their military strength. They went to Africa.

insights.claim: Paes-Braga calls the US move into Venezuela strategically 'prudent' — unlocking a mineral- and hydrocarbon-rich country with minimal collateral damage — and argues a stronger, stabilized Venezuela strengthens Latin America as a US-aligned resource base.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: If Venezuela/Latin America stabilizes under US influence, it opens a major new resource frontier; watch regional stability as a catalyst for minerals access.
insights.quote: I'd say a stronger Venezuela is a stronger Latin America.

insights.claim: The US lacks sufficient domestic mineral endowment (unlike its oil and gas position), so achieving mineral security requires capital formation plus international partnerships across Canada, Australia and Latin America — it cannot be solved within US borders alone.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: Domestic-only minerals plays are insufficient; the winning exposure includes allied-nation assets, not just US-soil projects.
insights.quote: America today does not have enough mineral endowment... they do not have enough mineral endowment within their borders.

insights.claim: Paes-Braga is skeptical that US mining 'exceptionalism' exists today — citing Freeport-McMoRan as the lone large US name but noting most of its operations are abroad and it has not delivered strong shareholder returns — implying the sector is wide open for a new winner.
insights.speaker: Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)
insights.so_what: The absence of a dominant, high-returning US miner is framed as the opportunity gap TMCR aims to fill.
insights.quote: Where's the exceptionalism in mining? Freeport McMoran. Well, okay.
